statement.en: Regulatory role of cell wall–modifying enzymes in fruit softening and texture development: A comprehensive review
statement.summary: Fruit softening is a defining feature of ripening that directly affects texture, sensory quality, shelf life, and postharvest losses. At the cellular level, softening results primarily from structural modifications of the primary cell wall and middle lamella. These changes are mediated by a coordinated network of cell wall–modifying enzymes that act on pectins, hemicelluloses, and cellulose, leading to weakening of cell-to-cell adhesion and wall integrity. Key enzymes involved in this process include polygalacturonase, pectin methylesterase, pectate lyase, β-galactosidase, cellulase, xyloglucan endotransglycosylase/hydrolase, and expansins. Their activities are tightly regulated by developmental signals, hormonal pathways—particularly ethylene—and environmental factors. This review provide

proposer: Lika, Rithy, Socheata, Chey, Sokra, In

The abstract is incomplete ('This review provide' truncates mid-sentence). The content itself—enzymatic mechanisms of fruit ripening—represents well-established plant biology, but the specific document's validation status is suspended pending publication and verification.
